text: And Absalom said: If thou wilt not come, at least let my brother Amnon, I beseech thee, come with us. And the king said to him: It is not necessary that he should go with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Absalom pressed him, so that he let Amnon and all the king’s sons go with him. And Absalom made a feast as it were the feast of a king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom had commanded his servants, saying: Take notice when Amnon shall be drunk with wine, and when I shall say to you: Strike him, and kill him, fear not: for it is I that command you: take courage, and be valiant men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the servants of Absalom did to Amnon as Absalom had commanded them. And all the king’s sons arose and got up every man upon his mule, and fled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And while they were yet in the way, a rumour came to David, saying: Absalom hath slain all the king’s sons, and there is not one of them left.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king rose up, and rent his garments: and fell upon the ground, and all his servants, that stood about him, rent their garments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Jonadab the son of Semmaa David’s brother answering, said: Let not my lord the king think that all the king’s sons are slain: Amnon only is dead, for he was appointed by the mouth of Absalom from the day that he ravished his sister Thamar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore let not my lord the king take this thing into his heart, saying: All the king’s sons are slain: for Amnon only is dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Absalom fled away: and the young man that kept the watch, lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold there came much people by a by-way on the side of the mountain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonadab said to the king: Behold the king’s sons are come: as thy servant said, so it is.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he made an end of speaking, the king’s sons also appeared: and coming in they lifted up their voice, and wept: and the king also and all his servants wept very much.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Absalom fled, and went to Tholomai the son of Ammiud the king of Gessur. And David mourned for his son every day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom after he was fled, and come into Gessur, was there three years. And king David ceased to pursue after Absalom, because he was comforted concerning the death of Amnon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab the son of Sarvia, understanding that the king’s heart was turned to Absalom,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sent to Thecua, and fetched from thence a wise woman: and said to her: Feign thyself to be a mourner, and put on mourning apparel, and be not anointed with oil, that thou mayest be as a woman that had a long time been mourning for one dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt go in to the king, and shalt speak to him in this manner. And Joab put the words in her mouth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the woman of Thecua was come in to the king, she fell before him upon the ground, and worshipped, and said: Save me, O king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to her: What is the matter with thee? She answered: Alas, I am a widow woman: for my husband is dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thy handmaid had two sons: and they quarrelled with each other in the field, and there was none to part them: and the one struck the other, and slew him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold the whole kindred rising against thy handmaid, saith: Deliver him that hath slain his brother, that we may kill him for the life of his brother, whom he slew, and that we may destroy the heir: and they seek to quench my spark which is left, and will leave my husband no name, nor remainder upon the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to the woman: Go to thy house, and I will give charge concerning thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the woman of Thecua said to the king: Upon me, my lord be the iniquity, and upon the house of my father: but may the king and his throne be guiltless.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said: If any one shall say ought against thee, bring him to me, and he shall not touch thee any more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she said: Let the king remember the Lord his God, that the next of kin be not multiplied to take revenge, and that they may not kill my son. And he said: As the Lord liveth, there shall not one hair of thy son fall to the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the woman said: Let thy hand maid speak one word to my lord the king. And he said: Speak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the woman said: Why hast thou thought such a thing against the people of God, and why hath the king spoken this word, to sin, and not bring home again his own exile?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We all die, and like waters that return no more, we fall down into the earth: neither will God have a soul to perish, but recalleth, meaning that he that is cast off should not altogether perish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore I am come, to speak this word to my lord the king before the people. And thy handmaid said: I will speak to the king, it maybe the king will perform the request of his handmaid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king hath hearkened to me to deliver his handmaid out of the hand of all that would destroy me and my son together out of the inheritance of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then let thy handmaid say, that the word of the Lord the king be made as a sacrifice. For even as an angel of God, so is my lord the king, that he is neither moved with blessing nor cursing: wherefore the Lord thy God is also with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king answering, said to the woman: Hide not from me the thing that I ask thee. And the woman said to him: Speak, my lord the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said: Is not the hand of Joab with thee in all this? The woman answered, and said: By the health of thy soul, my lord, O king, it is neither on the left hand, nor on the right, in all these things which my lord the king hath spoken: for thy servant Joab, he commanded me, and he put all these words into the mouth of thy handmaid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That I should come about with this form of speech, thy servant Joab commanded this: but thou, my lord, O king, art wise, according to the wisdom of an angel of God, to understand all things upon earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Joab: Behold I am appeased and have granted thy request: Go therefore and fetch back the boy Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab falling down to the ground upon his face, adored, and blessed the king: and Joab said: This day thy servant hath understood, that I have found grace in thy sight, my lord, O king: for thou hast fulfilled the request of thy servant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Joab arose and went to Gessur, and brought Absalom to Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the king said: Let him return into his house, and let him not see my face. So Absalom returned into his house, and saw not the king’s face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But in all Israel there was not a man so comely, and so exceedingly beautiful as Absalom: from the sole of the foot to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he polled his hair (now he was polled once a year, because his hair was burdensome to him) he weighed the hair of his head at two hundred sicles, according to the common weight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there were born to Absalom three sons: and one daughter, whose name was Thamar, and she was very beautiful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom dwelt two years in Jerusalem, and saw not the king’s face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sent therefore to Joab, to send him to the king: but he would not come to him. And when he had sent the second time, and he would not come to him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said to his servants: You know the field of Joab near my field, that hath a crop of barley: go now and set it on fire. So the servants of Absalom set the corn on fire. And Joab’s servants coming with their garments rent, said: The servants of Absalom have set part of the field on fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Joab arose, and came to Absalom to his house, and said: Why have thy servants set my corn on fire?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom answered Joab: I sent to thee beseeching thee to come to me, that I might send thee to the king, to say to him: Wherefore am I come from Gessur? it had been better for me to be there: I beseech thee therefore that I may see the face of the king: and if he be mindful of my iniquity, let him kill me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Joab going in to the king, told him all: and Absalom was called for, and, he went in to the king: and prostrated himself on the ground before him: and the king kissed Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now after these things Absalom made himself chariots, and horsemen, and fifty men to run before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom rising up early stood by the entrance of the gate, and when any man had business to come to the king’s judgment, Absalom called him to him, and said: Of what city art thou? He answered, and said: Thy servant is of such tribe of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom answered him: Thy words seem to me good and just. But there is no man appointed by the king to hear thee. And Absalom said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O that they would make me judge over the land, that all that have business might come to me, that I might do them justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover when any man came to him to salute him, he put forth his hand, and took him, and kissed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this he did to all Israel that came for judgment, to be heard by the king, and he enticed the hearts of the men of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after forty years, Absalom said to king David: Let me go, and pay my vows which I have vowed to the Lord in Hebron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy servant made a vow, when he was in Gessur of Syria, saying: If the Lord shall bring me again into Jerusalem, I will offer sacrifice to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king David said to him: Go in peace. And he arose, and went to Hebron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom sent spies into all the tribes of Israel, saying: As soon as you shall hear the sound of the trumpet, say ye: Absalom reigneth in Hebron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there went with Absalom two hundred men out of Jerusalem that were called, going with simplicity of heart, and knowing nothing of the design.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Absalom also sent for Achitophel the Gilonite, David’s counsellor, from his city Gilo. And while he was offering sacrifices, there was a strong conspiracy, and the people running together increased with Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there came a messenger to David, saying: All Israel with their whole heart followeth Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David said to his servants, that were with him in Jerusalem: Arise and let us flee: for we shall not escape else from the face of Absalom: make haste to go out, lest he come and overtake us, and bring ruin upon us, and smite the city with the edge of the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king’s servants said to him: Whatsoever our lord the king shall command, we thy servants will willingly execute.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king went forth, and all his household on foot: and the king left ten women his concubines to keep the house:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king going forth and all Israel on foot, stood afar off from the house:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all his servants walked by him, and the bands of the Cerethi, and the Phelethi, and all the Gethites, valiant warriors, six hundred men who had followed him from Geth on foot, went before the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Ethai the Gethite: Why comest thou with us: return and dwell with the king, for thou art a stranger, and art come out of thy own place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yesterday thou camest, and to day shalt thou be forced to go forth with us? but I shall go whither I am going: return thou, and take back thy brethren with thee, and the Lord will shew thee mercy, and truth, because thou hast shewn grace and fidelity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ethai answered the king, saying: As the Lord liveth, and as my lord the king liveth: in what place soever thou shalt be, my lord, O king, either in death, or in life, there will thy servant be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David said to Ethai: Come, and pass over. And Ethai the Gethite passed, and all the men that were with him, and the rest of the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they all wept with a loud voice, and all the people passed over: the king also himself went over the brook Cedron, and all the people marched towards the way that looketh to the desert.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Sadoc the priest also came, and all the Levites with him carrying the ark of the covenant of God, and they set down the ark of God: and Abiathar went up, till all the people that was come out of the city had done passing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Sadoc: Carry back the ark of God into the city: if I shall find grace in the sight of the Lord, he will bring me again, and he will shew me it, and his tabernacle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if he shall say to me: Thou pleasest me not: I am ready, let him do that which is good before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Sadoc the priest: O seer, return into the city in peace: and let Achimaas thy son, and Jonathan the son of Abiathar, your two sons, be with you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold I will lie hid in the plains of the wilderness, till there come word from you to certify me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Sadoc and Abiathar carried back the ark of God into Jerusalem: and they tarried there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But David went up by the ascent of mount Olivet, going up and weeping, walking barefoot, and with his head covered, and all the people that were with them, went up with their heads covered weeping.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was told David that Achitophel also was in the conspiracy with Absalom, and David said: Infatuate, O Lord, I beseech thee, the counsel of Achitophel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when David was come to the top of the mountain, where he was about to adore the Lord, behold Chusai the Arachite, came to meet him with his garment rent and his head covered with earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David said to him: If thou come with me, thou wilt be a burden to me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou return into the city, and wilt say to Absalom: I am thy servant, O king: as I have been thy father’s servant, so I will be thy servant: thou shalt defeat the counsel of Achitophel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou hast with thee Sadoc, and Abiathar the priests: and what thing soever thou shalt hear out of the king’s house, thou shalt tell it to Sadoc and Abiathar the priests.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there are with them their two sons Achimaas; the son of Sadoc, and Jonathan the son of Abiathar: and you shall send by them to me every thing that you shall hear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Chusai the friend of David went into the city, and Absalom came into Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when David was a little past the top of the hill, behold Siba the servant of Miphiboseth came to meet him with two asses, laden with two hundred loaves of bread, and a hundred bunches of raisins, a hundred cakes of figs, and a vessel of wine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Siba: What mean these things? And Siba answered: The asses are for the king’s household to sit on: and the loaves and the figs for thy servants to eat, and the wine to drink if any man be faint in the desert.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said: Where is thy master’s son? And Siba answered the king: He remained in Jerusalem, saying: To day, will the house of Israel restore me the kingdom of my father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Siba: I give thee all that belonged to Miphiboseth. And Siba said: I beseech thee let me find grace before thee, my lord, O king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king David came as far as Bahurim: and behold there came out from thence a man of the kindred of the house of Saul named Semei, the son of Gera, and coming out he cursed as he went on,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he threw stones at David, and at all the servants of king David: and all the people, and all the warriors walked on the right, and on the left side of the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thus said Semei when he cursed the king: Come out, come out, thou man of blood, and thou man of Belial.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord hath repaid thee for all the blood of the house of Saul: because thou hast usurped the kingdom in his stead, and the Lord hath given the kingdom into the hand of Absalom thy son: and behold thy evils press upon thee, because thou art a man of blood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Abisai the son of Sarvia said to the king: Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? I will go, and cut off his head.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said: What have I to do with you, ye sons of Sarvia? Let him alone and let him curse: for the Lord hath bid him curse David: and who is he that shall dare say, why hath he done so?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Abisai, and to all his servants: Behold my son, who came forth from my bowels, seeketh my life: how much more now a son of Jemini? let him alone that he may curse as the Lord hath bidden him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Perhaps the Lord may look upon my affliction, and the Lord may render me good for the cursing of this day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David and his men with him went by the way. And Semei by the hill’s side went over against him, cursing, and casting stones at him, and scattering earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king and all the people with him came weary, and refreshed themselves there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Absalom and all his people came into Jerusalem, and Achitophel was with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Chusai the Arachite, David’s friend, was come to Absalom, he said to him: God save thee, O king, God save thee, O king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom said to him, Is this thy kindness to thy friend? Why wentest thou not with thy friend?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Chusai answered Absalom: Nay: for I will be his, whom the Lord hath chosen, and all this people, and all Israel, and with him will I abide.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Besides this, whom shall I serve? is it not the king’s son? as I have served thy father, so will I serve thee also.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom said to Achitophel: Consult what we are to do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Achitophel said to Absalom: Go in to the concubines of thy father, whom he hath left to keep the house: that when all Israel shall hear that thou hast disgraced thy father, their hands may be strengthened with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they spread a tent for Absalom on the top of the house, and he went in to his father’s concubines before all Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the counsel of Achitophel, which he gave in those days, was as if a man should consult God: so was all the counsel of Achitophel, both when he was with David, and when he was with Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Achitophel said to Absalom: I will choose me twelve thousand men, and I will arise and pursue after David this night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And coming upon him (for he is now weary, and weak handed) I will defeat him: and when all the people is put to flight that is with him, I will kill the king who will be left alone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will bring back all the people, as if they were but one man: for thou seekest but one man: and all the people shall be in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his saying pleased Absalom, and all the ancients of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Absalom said: Call Chusai the Arachite, and let us hear what he also saith.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Chusai was come to Absalom, Absalom said to him: Achitophel hath spoken after this manner: shall we do it or not? what counsel dost thou give?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Chusai said to Absalom: The counsel that Achitophel hath given this time is not good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And again Chusai said: Thou knowest thy father, and the men that are with him, that they are very valiant, and bitter in their mind, as a bear raging in the wood when her whelps are taken away: and thy father is a warrior, and will not lodge with the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Perhaps he now lieth hid in pits, or in some other place where he list: and when any one shall fall at the first, every one that heareth it shall say: There is a slaughter among the people that followed Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the most valiant man whose heart is as the heart of a lion, shall melt for fear: for all the people of Israel know thy father to be a valiant man, and that all who are with him are valiant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But this seemeth to me to be good counsel: Let all Israel be gathered to thee, from Dan to Bersabee, as the sand of the sea which cannot be numbered: and thou shalt be in the midst of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we shall come upon him in what place soever he shall be found: and we shall cover him, as the dew falleth upon the ground, and we shall not leave of the men that are with him, not so much as one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if he shall enter into any city, all Israel shall cast ropes round about that city, and we will draw it into the river, so that there shall not be found so much as one small stone thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Absalom, and all the men of Israel said: The counsel of Chusai the Arachite is better than the counsel of Achitophel: and by the will of the Lord the profitable counsel of Achitophel was defeated, that the Lord might bring evil upon Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Chusai said to Sadoc and Abiathar the priests: Thus and thus did Achitophel counsel Absalom, and the ancients of Israel: and thus and thus did I counsel them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore send quickly, and tell David, saying: Tarry not this night in the plains of the wilderness, but without delay pass over: lest the king be swallowed up, and all the people that is with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jonathan and Achimaas stayed by the fountain Rogel: and there went a maid and told them: and they went forward, to carry the message to king David, for they might not be seen, nor enter into the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But a certain boy saw them, and told Absalom: but they making haste went into the house of a certain man in Bahurim, who had a well in his court, and they went down into it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a woman took, and spread a covering over the mouth of the well, as it were to dry sodden barley and so the thing was not known.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Absalom’s servants were come into the house, they said to the woman: Where is Achimaas and Jonathan? and the woman answered them: They passed on in haste, after they had tasted a little water. But they that sought them, when they found them not, returned into Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were gone, they came up out of the well, and going on told king David, and said: Arise, and pass quickly over the river: for this manner of counsel has Achitophel given against you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So David arose, and all the people that were with him, and they passed over the Jordan, until it grew light, and not one of them was left that was not gone over the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Achitophel seeing that his counsel was not followed, saddled his ass, and arose and went home to his house and to his city, and putting his house in order, hanged himself, and was buried in the sepulchre of his father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But David came to the camp, and Absalom passed over the Jordan, he and all the men of Israel with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Absalom appointed Amasa in Joab’s stead over the army: and Amasa was the son of a man who was called Jethra, of Jezrael, who went in to Abigail the daughter of Naas, the sister of Sarvia who was the mother of Joab.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Israel camped with Absalom in the land of Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when David was come to the camp, Sobi the son of Naas of Rabbath of the children of Ammon, and Machir the son of Ammihel of Lodabar and Berzellai the Galaadite of Rogelim,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Brought him beds, and tapestry, and earthen vessels, and wheat, and barley, and meal, and parched corn, and beans, and lentils, and fried pulse,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And honey, and butter, and sheep, and fat calves, and they gave to David and the people that were with him, to eat: for they suspected that the people were faint with hunger and thirst in the wilderness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David, having reviewed his people, appointed over them captains of thousands and of hundreds,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And sent forth a third part of the people under the hand of Joab, and a third part under the hand of Abisai the son of Sarvia Joab’s brother, and a third part under the hand of Ethai, who was of Geth: and the king said to the people: I also will go forth with you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the people answered: Thou shalt not go forth: for if we flee away, they will not much mind us: or if half of us should fall, they will not greatly care: for thou alone art accounted for ten thousand: it is better therefore that thou shouldst be in the city to succour us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to them: What seemeth good to you, that will I do. And the king stood by the gate: and all the people went forth by their troops, by hundreds and by thousands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king commanded Joab, and Abisai, and Ethai, saying: Save me the boy Absalom. And all the people heard the king giving charge to all the princes concerning Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the people went out into the field against Israel, and the battle was fought in the forest of Ephraim.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the people of Israel were defeated there by David’s army, and a great slaughter was made that day of twenty thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the battle there was scattered over the face of all the country, and there were many more of the people whom the forest consumed, than whom the sword devoured that day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it happened that Absalom met the servants of David, riding on a mule: and as the mule went under a thick and large oak, his head stuck in the oak: and while he hung between the heaven and the earth, the mule on which he rode passed on.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And one saw this and told Joab, saying: I saw Absalom hanging upon an oak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab said to the man that told him: If thou sawest him, why didst thou not stab him to the ground, and I would have given thee ten sicles of silver, and a belt?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to Joab: If thou wouldst have paid down in my hands a thousand pieces of silver, I would not lay my hands upon the king’s son for in our hearing the king charged thee, and Abisai, and Ethai, saying: Save me the boy Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea and if I should have acted boldly against my own life, this could not have been hid from the king, and wouldst thou have stood by me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab said: Not as thou wilt, but I will set upon him in thy sight. So he took three lances in his hand, and thrust them into the heart of Absalom: and whilst he yet panted for life, sticking on the oak,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ten young men, armourbearers of Joab, ran up, and striking him slew him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab sounded the trumpet, and kept back the people from pursuing after Israel in their flight, being willing to spare the multitude.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took Absalom, and cast him into a great pit in the forest, and they laid an exceeding great heap of stones upon him: but all Israel fled to their own dwellings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Absalom had reared up for himself, in his lifetime, a pillar, which is in the king’s valley: for he said: I have no son, and this shall be the monument of my name. And he called the pillar by his own name, and it is called the hand of Absalom, to this day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Achimaas the son of Sadoc said: I will run and tell the king, that the Lord hath done judgment for him from the hand of his enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab said to him: Thou shalt not be the messenger this day, but shalt bear tidings another day: this day I will not have thee bear tidings, because the king’s son is dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab said to Chusai: Go, and tell the king what thou hast seen. Chusai bowed down to Joab, and ran.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Achimaas the son of Sadoc said to Joab again: Why might not I also run after Chusai? And Joab said to him: Why wilt thou run, my son? thou wilt not be the bearer of good tidings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered: But what if I run? And he said to him: Run. Then Achimaas running by a nearer way passed Chusai.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David sat between the two gates: and the watchman that was on the top of the gate upon the wall, lifting up his eyes, saw a man running alone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And crying out he told the king: and the king said: If he be alone, there are good tidings in his mouth. And as he was coming apace, and drawing nearer,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The watchman saw another man running, and crying aloud from above, he said: I see another man running alone. And the king said: He also is a good messenger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the watchman said: The running of the foremost seemeth to me like the running of Achimaas the son of Sadoc. And the king said: He is a good man: and cometh with good news.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Achimaas crying out, said to the king: God save thee, O king. And falling down before the king with his face to the ground, he said: Blessed be the Lord thy God, who hath shut up the men that have lifted up their hands against the lord my king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said: Is the young man Absalom safe? And Achimaas said: I saw a great tumult, O king, when thy servant Joab sent me thy servant: I know nothing else.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to him: Pass, and stand here.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had passed, and stood still, Chusai appeared and coming up he said: I bring good tidings, my lord, the king, for the Lord hath judged for thee this day from the hand of all that have risen up against thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Chusai: Is the young man Absalom safe? And Chusai answering him, said: Let the enemies of my lord, the king, and all that rise against him unto evil, be as the young man is.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king therefore being much moved, went up to the high chamber over the gate, and wept. And as he went he spoke in this manner: My son Absalom, Absalom my son: would to God that I might die for thee, Absalom my son, my son Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was told Joab, that the king wept and mourned for his son:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the victory that day was turned into mourning unto all the people: for the people heard say that day: The king grieveth for his son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the people shunned the going into the city that day as a people would do that hath turned their backs, and fled away from the battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king covered his head, and cried with a loud voice: O my son Absalom, O Absalom my son, O my son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Joab going into the house to the king, said: Thou hast shamed this day the faces of all thy servants, that have saved thy life, and the lives of thy sons, and of thy daughters, and the lives of thy wives, and the lives of thy concubines.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou lovest them that hate thee, and thou hatest them that love thee: and thou hast shewn this day that thou carest not for thy nobles, nor for thy servants: and I now plainly perceive that if Absalom had lived, and all we had been slain, then it would have pleased thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore arise, and go out, and speak to the satisfaction of thy servants: for I swear to thee by the Lord, that if thou wilt not go forth, there will not tarry with thee so much as one this night: and that will be worse to thee, than all the evils that have befallen thee from thy youth until now.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king arose and sat in the gate: and it was told to all the people that the king sat in the gate: and all the people came before the king, but Israel fled to their own dwellings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the people were at strife in all the tribes of Israel, saying: The king delivered us out of the hand of our enemies, and he saved us out of the hand of the Philistines: and now he is fled out of the land for Absalom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Absalom, whom we anointed over us, is dead in the battle: how long are you silent, and bring not back the king?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And king David sent to Sadoc, and Abiathar the priests, saying: Speak to the ancients of Juda, saying: Why are you the last to bring the king back to his house? (For the talk of all Israel was come to the king in his house.)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You are my brethren, you are my bone, and my flesh, why are you the last to bring back the king?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And say ye to Amasa: Art not thou my bone, and my flesh? So do God to me and add more, if thou be not the chief captain of the army before me always in the place of Joab.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he inclined the heart of all the men of Juda, as it were of one man: and they sent to the king, saying: Return thou, and all thy servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king returned and came as far as the Jordan, and all Juda came as far as Galgal to meet the king, and to bring him over the Jordan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Semei the son of Gera the son of Jemini of Bahurim, made haste and went down with the men of Juda to meet king David,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With a thousand men of Benjamin, and Siba the servant of the house of Saul: and his fifteen sons, and twenty servants were with him: and going over the Jordan,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They passed the fords before the king, that they might help over the king’s household, and do according to his commandment. And Semei the son of Gera falling down before the king, when he was come over the Jordan,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Said to him: Impute not to me, my lord, the iniquity, nor remember the injuries of thy servant on the day that thou, my lord, the king, wentest out of Jerusalem, nor lay it up in thy heart, O king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I thy servant acknowledge my sin: and therefore I am come this day the first of all the house of Joseph, and am come down to meet my lord the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Abisai the son of Sarvia answering, said: Shall Semei for these words not be put to death, because he cursed the Lord’s anointed?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David said: What have I to do with you, ye sons of Sarvia? why are you a satan this day to me? shall there any man be killed this day in Israel? do not I know that this day I am made king over Israel?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Semei: Thou shalt not die. And he swore unto him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Miphiboseth the son of Saul came down to meet the king, and he had neither washed his feet, nor trimmed his beard: nor washed his garments from the day that the king went out, until the day of his return in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he met the king at Jerusalem, the king said to him: Why camest thou not with me, Miphiboseth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answering, said: My lord, O king, my servant despised me: for I thy servant spoke to him to saddle me an ass, that I might get on and go with the king: for I thy servant am lame.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover he hath also accused me thy servant to thee, my lord the king: but thou my lord the king art as an angel of God, do what pleaseth thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all of my father’s house were no better than worthy of death before my lord the king; and thou hast set me thy servant among the guests of thy table: what just complaint therefore have I? or what right to cry any more to the king?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king said to him: Why speakest thou any more? what I have said is determined: thou and Siba divide the possessions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Miphiboseth answered the king: Yea, let him take all, forasmuch as my lord the king is returned peaceably into his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Berzellai also the Galaadite coming down from Rogelim, brought the king over the Jordan, being ready also to wait on him beyond the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Berzellai the Galaadite was of a great age, that is to say, fourscore years old, and he provided the king with sustenance when he abode in the camp: for he was a man exceeding rich.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Berzellai: Come with me that thou mayest rest secure with me in Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Berzellai said to the king: How many are the days of the years of my life, that I should go up with the king to Jerusalem?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am this day fourscore years old, are my senses quick to discern sweet and bitter? or can meat or drink delight thy servant? or can I hear any more the voice of singing men and singing women? why should thy servant be a burden to my lord, the king?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I thy servant will go on a little way from the Jordan with thee: I need not this recompense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But I beseech thee let thy servant return, and die in my own city, and be buried by the sepulchre of my father, and of my mother. But there is thy servant Chamaam, let him go with thee, my lord, the king, and do to him whatsoever seemeth good to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king said to him: Let Chamaam go over with me, and I will do for him whatsoever shall please thee, and all that thou shalt ask of me, thou shalt obtain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when all the people and the king had passed over the Jordan, the king kissed Berzellai, and blessed him: and he returned to his own place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the king went on to Galgal, and Chamaam with him. Now all the people of Juda had brought the king over, and only half of the people of Israel were there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore all the men of Israel running together to the king, said to him: Why have our brethren the men of Juda stolen thee away, and have brought the king and his household over the Jordan, and all the men of David with him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the men of Juda answered the men of Israel: Because the king is nearer to me: why art thou angry for this matter? have we eaten any thing of the king’s, or have any gifts been given us?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the men of Israel answered the men of Juda, and said: I have ten parts in the king more than thou, and David belongeth to me more than to thee: why hast thou done me a wrong, and why was it not told me first, that I might bring back my king? And the men of Juda answered more harshly than the men of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there happened to be there a man of Belial, whose name was Seba, the son of Bochri, a man of Jemini: and he sounded the trumpet, and said: We have no part in David, nor inheritance in the son of Isai: return to thy dwellings, O Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all Israel departed from David, and followed Seba the son of Bochri: but the men of Juda stuck to their king from the Jordan unto Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the king was come into his house at Jerusalem, he took the ten women his concubines, whom he had left to keep the house, and put them inward, allowing them provisions: and he went not in unto them, but they were shut up unto the day of their death living in widowhood.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to Amasa: Assemble to me all the men of Juda against the third day, and be thou here present.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Amasa went to assemble the men of Juda, but he tarried beyond the set time which the king had appointed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David said to Abisai: Now will Seba the son of Bochri do us more harm than did Absalom: take thou therefore the servants of thy lord, and pursue after him, lest he find fenced cities, and escape us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Joab’s men went out with him, and the Cerethi and the Phelethi: and all the valiant men went out of Jerusalem to pursue after Seba the son of Bochri.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were at the great stone which is in Gabaon, Amasa coming met them. And Joab had on a close coat of equal length with his habit, and over it was girded with a sword hanging down to his flank, in a scabbard, made in such manner as to come out with the least motion and strike.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab said to Amasa: God save thee, my brother. And he took Amasa by the chin with his right hand to kiss him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Amasa did not take notice of the sword, which Joab had, and he struck him in the side, and shed out his bowels to the ground, and gave him not a second wound, and he died. And Joab, and Abisai his brother pursued after Seba the son of Bochri.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the mean time some men of Joab’s company stopping at the dead body of Amasa, said: Behold he that would have been in Joab’s stead the companion of David.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Amasa imbrued with blood, lay in the midst of the way. A certain man saw this that all the people stood still to look upon him, so he removed Amasa out of the highway into the field, and covered him with a garment, that they who passed might, not stop on his account.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was removed out of the way, all the people went on following Joab to pursue after Seba the son of Bochri.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now he had passed through all the tribes of Israel unto Abela and Bethmaacha: and all the chosen men were gathered together unto him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they came, and besieged him in Abela, and in Bethmaacha, and they cast up works round the city, and the city was besieged: and all the people that were with Joab, laboured to throw down the walls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a wise woman cried out from the city: Hear, hear, and say to Joab: Come near hither, and I will speak with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was come near to her, she said to him: Art thou Joab? And he answered: I am. And she spoke thus to him: Hear the words of thy handmaid. He answered: I do hear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she again said: A saying was used in the old proverb: They that inquire, let them inquire in Abela: and so they made an end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Am not I she that answer truth in Israel, and thou seekest to destroy the city, and to overthrow a mother in Israel? Why wilt thou throw down the inheritance of the Lord?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Joab answering said: God forbid, God forbid that I should, I do not throw down, nor destroy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The matter is not so, but a man of mount Ephraim, Seba the son of Bochri by name, hath lifted up his hand against king David: deliver him only, and we will depart from the city. And the woman said to Joab: Behold his head shall be thrown to thee from the wall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So she went to all the people, and spoke to them wisely: and they cut off the head of Seba the son of Bochri, and cast it out to Joab. And he sounded the trumpet, and they departed from the city, every one to their home: and Joab returned to Jerusalem to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Joab was over all the army of Israel: and Banaias the son of Joiada was over the Cerethites and Phelethites,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Aduram over the tributes: and Josaphat the son of Ahilud was recorder.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Siva was scribe: and Sadoc and Abiathar, priests.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Ira the Jairite was the priest of David.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there was a famine in the days of David for three years successively: and David consulted the oracle of the Lord. And the Lord said: It is for Saul, and his bloody house, because he slew the Gabaonites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king, calling for the Gabaonites, said to them: (Now the Gabaonites were not of the children of Israel, but the remains of the Amorrhites: and the children of Israel had sworn to them, and Saul sought to slay them out of zeal, as it were for the children of Israel and Juda:)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: David therefore said to the Gabaonites: What shall I do for you? and what shall be the atonement for you, that you may bless the inheritance of the Lord?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Gabaonites said to him: We have no contest about silver and gold, but against Saul and against his house: neither do we desire that any man be slain of Israel. And the king said to them: What will you then that I should do for you?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said to the king: The man that crushed us and oppressed us unjustly, we must destroy in such manner that there be not so much as one left of his stock in all the coasts of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let seven men of his children be delivered unto us, that we may crucify them to the Lord in Gabaa of Saul, once the chosen of the Lord. And the king said: I will give them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king spared Miphiboseth the son of Jonathan the son of Saul, because of the oath of the Lord, that had been between David and Jonathan the son of Saul.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the king took the two sons of Respha the daughter of Aia, whom she bore to Saul, Armoni, and Miphiboseth: and the five sons of Michol the daughter of Saul, whom she bore to Hadriel the son of Berzellai, that was of Molathi:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And gave them into the hands of the Gabaonites: and they crucified them on a hill before the Lord: and these seven died together in the first days of the harvest, when the barley began to be reaped.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Respha the daughter of Aia took haircloth, and spread it under her upon the rock from the beginning of the harvest, till water dropped upon them out of heaven: and suffered neither the birds to tear them by day, nor the beasts by night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was told David, what Respha the daughter of Aia, the concubine of Saul, had done.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David went, and took the bones of Saul, and the bones of Jonathan his son from the men of Jabes Galaad, who had stolen them from the street of Bethsan, where the Philistines had hanged them when they had slain Saul in Gelboe.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he brought from thence the bones of Saul, and the bones of Jonathan his son, and they gathered up the bones of them that were crucified,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they buried them with the bones of Saul, and of Jonathan his son in the land of Benjamin, in the side, in the sepulchre of Cis his father: and they did all that the king had commanded, and God shewed mercy again to the land after these things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Philistines made war again against Israel, and David went down, and his servants with him, and fought against the Philistines. And David growing faint,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Jesbibenob, who was of the race of Arapha, the iron of whose spear weighed three hundred ounces, being girded with a new sword, attempted to kill David.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Abisai the son of Sarvia rescued him, and striking the Philistine killed him. Then David’s men swore unto him saying: Thou shalt go no more out with us to battle, lest thou put out the lamp of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There was also a second battle in Gob against the Philistines: then Sobochai of Husathi slew Saph of the race of Arapha of the family of the giants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there was a third battle in Gob against the Philistines, in which Adeodatus the son of the Forrest an embroiderer of Bethlehem slew Goliath the Gethite, the shaft of whose spear was like a weaver’s beam.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fourth battle was in Geth: where there was a man of great stature, that had six fingers on each hand, and six toes on each foot, four and twenty in all, and he was of the race of Arapha.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he reproached Israel: and Jonathan the son of Samae the brother of David slew him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These four were born of Arapha in Geth, and they fell by the hand of David, and of his servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And David spoke to the Lord the words of this canticle, in the day that the Lord delivered him out of the hand of all his enemies, and out of the hand of Saul,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: The Lord is my rock, and my strength, and my saviour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God is my strong one, in him will I trust: my shield, and the horn of my salvation: he lifteth me up, and is my refuge: my saviour, thou wilt deliver me from iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will call on the Lord who is worthy to be praised: and I shall be saved from my enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the pangs of death have surrounded me: the floods of Belial have made me afraid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The cords of hell compassed me: the snares of death prevented me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In my distress I will call upon the Lord, and I will cry to my God: and he will hear my voice out of his temple, and my cry shall come to his ears.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The earth shook and trembled, the foundations of the mountains were moved, and shaken, because he was angry with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A smoke went up from his nostrils, and a devouring fire out of his mouth: coals were kindled by it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He bowed the heavens, and came down: and darkness was under his feet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he rode upon the cherubims, and flew: and slid upon the wings of the wind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He made darkness a covering round about him: dropping waters out of the clouds of the heavens.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By the brightness before him, the coals of fire were kindled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord shall thunder from heaven: and the most high shall give forth his voice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He shot arrows and scattered them: lightning, and consumed them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the overflowings of the sea appeared, and the foundations of the world were laid open at the rebuke of the Lord, at the blast of the spirit of his wrath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sent from on high, and took me, and drew me out of many waters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He delivered me from my most mighty enemy, and from them that hated me: for they were too strong for me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He prevented me in the day of my affliction, and the Lord became my stay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he brought me forth into a large place, he delivered me, because I pleased him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord will reward me according to my justice: and according to the cleanness of my hands he will render to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because I have kept the ways of the Lord, and have not wickedly departed from my God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For all his judgments are in my sight: and his precepts I have not removed from me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I shall be perfect with him: and shall keep myself from my iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord will recompense me according to my justice: and according to the cleanness of my hands in the sight of his eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With the holy one thou wilt be holy: and with the valiant perfect.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With the elect thou wilt be elect: and with the perverse thou wilt be perverted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the poor people thou wilt save: and with thy eyes thou shalt humble the haughty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou art my lamp O Lord: and thou, O Lord, wilt enlighten my darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For in thee I will run girded: in my God I will leap over the wall.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God, his way is immaculate, the word of the Lord is tried by fire: he is the shield of all that trust in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is God but the Lord: and who is strong but our God?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God who hath girded me with strength, and made my way perfect.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Making my feet like the feet of harts, and setting me upon my high places.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He teacheth my hands to war: and maketh my arms like a bow of brass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast given me the shield of my salvation: and thy mildness hath multiplied me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt enlarge my steps under me: and my ankles shall not fail.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will pursue after my enemies, and crush them: and will not return again till I consume them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will consume them and break them in pieces, so that they shall not rise: they shall fall under my feet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast girded me with strength to battle: thou hast made them that resisted me to bow under me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My enemies thou hast made to turn their back to me: them that hated me, and I shall destroy them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall cry, and there shall be none to save: to the Lord, and he shall not hear them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I shall beat them as small as the dust of the earth: I shall crush them and spread them abroad like the mire of the streets.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou wilt save me from the contradictions of my people: thou wilt keep me to be the head of the Gentiles: the people which I know not, shall serve me,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sons of the stranger will resist me, at the hearing of the ear they will obey me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The strangers are melted away, and shall be straitened in their distresses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord liveth, and my God is blessed: and the strong God of my salvation shall be exalted:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God who giveth me revenge, and bringest down people under me,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who bringest me forth from my enemies, and liftest me up from them that resist me: from the wicked man thou shalt deliver me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore will I give thanks to thee, O Lord, among the Gentiles, and will sing to thy name.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Giving great salvation to his king, and shewing mercy to David his anointed, and to his seed for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now these are David’s last words. David the son of Isai said: The man to whom it was appointed concerning the Christ of the God of Jacob, the excellent psalmist of Israel said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The spirit of the Lord hath spoken by me and his word by my tongue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The God of Israel said to me, the strong one of Israel spoke, the ruler of men, the just ruler in the fear of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the light of the morning, when the sun riseth, shineth in the morning without clouds, and as the grass springeth out of the earth by rain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither is my house so great with God, that he should make with me an eternal covenant, firm in all things and assured. For he is all my salvation, and all my will: neither is there ought thereof that springeth not up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But transgressors shall all of them be plucked up as thorns: which are not taken away with hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if a man will touch them, he must be armed with iron and with the staff of a lance: but they shall be set on fire and burnt to nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the names of the valiant men of David: Jesbaham sitting in the chair was the wisest chief among the three, he was like the most tender little worm of the wood, who killed eight hundred men at one onset.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After him was Eleazar the son of Dodo the Ahohite, one of the three valiant men that were with David when they defied the Philistines, and they were there gathered together to battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
